does: This paper investigates how value alignment emerges in large language models during post-training, rather than only evaluating alignment in finished models. The authors conduct experiments with Llama-3 and Qwen-3 models across different sizes, systematically isolating the effects of post-training algorithms (supervised fine-tuning and preference optimization) and training datasets. They measure both the magnitude and timing of value shifts, tracking when and how models learn to express human values throughout training. A key empirical finding is that the supervised fine-tuning phase establishes the model's core values, while subsequent preference optimization steps rarely substantially re-align these foundational values. The work uses synthetic preference data to probe and understand these dynamics, providing a granular picture of value learning trajectories rather than static end-state assessments.
connects: This work extends the growing literature on LLM alignment and value learning, building on prior evaluations of trained model alignment that typically treat models as static endpoints. It responds to the need for deeper understanding of training dynamics in alignment, a direction increasingly recognized as important for interpretability and safety. The paper combines supervised fine-tuning and preference optimization methods—the dominant post-training approaches in recent LLM development—examining how these distinct algorithmic paradigms contribute differently to value emergence. It also relates to broader research into when and how neural networks learn different types of representations, adapting insights from neural network training dynamics to the alignment context. The use of controlled datasets and synthetic preferences builds on experimental methodology developed in interpretability and alignment evaluation work.
enables: This research provides foundations for improving post-training strategies by clarifying which stages and methods most shape model values. Understanding that SFT establishes core values while preference optimization has limited re-alignment power suggests potential interventions for more robust and controllable alignment during training. The findings could inform better dataset curation and algorithm design for post-training, potentially allowing earlier and more efficient value instillation. The methodology of tracing value drift through training stages could be extended to probe other model behaviors and capabilities, contributing to broader interpretability research. This work also enables more targeted research into value stability and potential adversarial value shifting, and suggests directions for understanding how different alignment techniques could be combined more effectively to achieve desired value configurations in deployed systems.
